turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Breyer has the opinion this morning in case 15-649, Czyzewski versus Jevic Holding Corp.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: This is a very technical case. Chapter 11 of the Bankruptcy Code lets debtors and creditors negotiate a plan where they hope a company will survive and it can involve dividing assets among various creditors and so forth, but sometimes, they cannot agree on the plan and when they do not, the bankruptcy court may dismiss the Chapter 11 proceeding. The Code then, says well when they dismiss it ordinarily, you sort of try to restore the different parties to their pre-bankruptcy petition situation. In other words, there is a state sort of divided up and try to put them back to square one. Now, it also the code set forth a system of priorities. Now, the priorities are when you give out the money of the debtor, who gets the money first? And usually, it is the secured creditors and then at the bottom are the shareholders, and then in between there are whole bunch of other creditors possibly listed in a certain order. Now, where you have a final division of assets as it called Chapter 7 and Chapter 11 Plan, you have to follow that certain system of priorities. But what happens to the money when you dismiss the Chapter 11 because nobody could agree? I just said, you try to restore them to square one, but is not quite so and this is about that problem. Suppose that the bankruptcy judge does not restore them to one but for various reasons here because they had settled a lawsuit and they were only going to settle it on certain conditions and he divided some of that money left and he gave the money to the top tier, the secured creditors and to the bottom tier, but they left out the middle tier, which is just what the people who settled the case wanted them to do. Now, can you do that? Because, in the part of the code that lists what happens when you dismiss the Chapter 11, it does not say anything really about priorities; it has a whole bunch of provisions. So, now you have got the question. Can you depart from this ordinary priority system when he dismisses the case what's called, if you want to be technical sometimes, what is it, a structured dismissal or something like that? All right, you got the question. Now, it's complicated. The answer is simple, no. The answer is no, he cannot depart from the ordinary priority systems and our reasons basically are this priority system is very important to the whole Bankruptcy Code, and if Congress wanted the departure in this situation, they would have said more about it. It does say, you can depart for cause, but if you look at it, that for cause probably means in our opinion, you can depart to try to make up for things that happened unfairly or whatever it is during the proceeding, but not a big departure like this one. And so, that's basically the answer we come to. Our basic reason is because of the importance and we give a lot of other reasons too in the opinion, which is even more technical. Now, Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justice Alito has joined.
